topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an unnerving intensity, casting sharp shadows across the polished linoleum floor. Liam clutched the crumpled application form, his knuckles white against the cheap paper. He kept glancing at Sarah, who was casually leaning against the water cooler, a serene, almost unnerving calm radiating from her. Every time she laughed at something a colleague said, a cold knot tightened in Liam's stomach, making his breath catch in his throat. He reread the job description, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. A tremor ran through his hand as he tried to smooth the creases, certain that if he looked up, Sarah would see the sheer panic churning beneath his composure. He needed this job, desperately, but the proximity of her easy confidence made him feel utterly exposed, like a specimen under a microscope.

[story 2]

“Are you sure about this, Maya?” Ben asked, his voice tight, as he watched Clara meticulously arrange her portfolio. The air in the waiting room felt thick, suffocating, pressing down on his chest until it was hard to draw a full breath. He kept shifting his weight from one foot to the other, the cheap carpet seeming to stick to his soles. Every time Clara glanced his way, a sudden surge of heat rushed to his face, and he quickly looked down at his shoes, pretending intense interest in the scuff marks. The thought that she might secure the position, while he remained stuck in this anxious limbo, sent a wave of sheer dread washing over him. He wanted to bolt, to disappear into the crowded hallway, but his feet felt cemented to the spot.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen, my vision tunneling. The 'Submit Application' button seemed impossibly far away, a distant, unreachable star. My palms were slick; I could feel the dampness seeping through my skin. Across the desk, Alex was sketching idly in a notebook, his movements slow and deliberate, completely at odds with the frantic drumming inside my own skull. When I finally forced my finger down, the click sounded deafening in the silence. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, trapped bird. I didn't dare look up. The silence stretched, taut and brittle, and I waited, every muscle coiled, anticipating some catastrophic judgment from the system, or perhaps from Alex, whose unreadable expression seemed to hold all the answers I desperately needed.
